title: Zereshk polo
subtitle: Persian barberry and saffron rice with chicken
excerpt: Saffron-tinted basmati rice layered with tart dried barberries and served with a whole braised chicken or chicken pieces. The barberries (zereshk in Farsi) are cooked in butter with a touch of sugar to plump them up and soften their sourness, then folded through the rice. One of Iran's most celebrated dishes.
sourceNotes: Zereshk polo ba morgh (barberry rice with chicken) is one of the classic dishes of Persian cooking, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and in Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). Barberries (zereshk) are small tart dried berries used in Persian cooking, sold in Middle Eastern shops. Saffron is essential, the golden colour is part of the dish's identity.

content.text: The rice is cooked using the Persian two-stage method: parboiled until barely cooked through, then drained and steamed in a covered pot with butter to form a tahdig crust. The barberries are sautéed separately in butter with a small amount of sugar, just enough to take the sharpest edge off their tartness. They are folded into the rice at the end or used as a topping. The saffron water is spooned over the top layer of rice after mounding, turning the top a deep golden.

The chicken is braised separately in a simple sauce of onion, turmeric, and saffron. It can be cooked whole or as jointed pieces; bone-in thighs work well and produce a more intensely flavoured sauce. The braising liquid reduces to a concentrated sauce spooned over the chicken when serving. Serve the rice with the chicken pieces placed on top and the barberry mixture across the centre.

items.name: Basmati rice
items.prepNote: rinsed and soaked in salted water for 1 hour

items.name: Saffron
items.prepNote: steeped in 2 tbsp hot water, for the chicken

content.text: Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a wide pan. Brown the {{chicken-thigh}} pieces for 3 to 4 minutes per side. Remove. Cook the {{onion}} in the same pan for 10 minutes until soft. Add the {{turmeric}} and saffron water and stir for 1 minute. Return the chicken, add 200 ml water, cover, and cook for 35 to 40 minutes until very tender. Uncover and reduce the sauce until it coats the chicken. Season with salt.

Drain the {{basmati-rice}}. Parboil in a large pot of well-salted boiling water for 5 to 6 minutes until just cooked on the outside but firm in the centre. Drain. In the same pot, melt 40 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} over medium-high heat with 2 tablespoons of water. Mound the rice into the pot, poke holes with a spoon handle, wrap the lid in a tea towel, and cover. Cook on medium-high for 5 minutes, then reduce to the lowest heat and cook for 30 to 35 minutes until a crust forms. Spoon the rice saffron water over the top layer.

Melt the remaining 20 g {{unsalted-butter}} in a small pan. Add the {{barberry}} and {{caster-sugar}} and cook for 2 to 3 minutes until plump. To serve: scoop the rice onto a platter, spread the barberry mixture across the centre. Place the chicken on top. Spoon over the braising juices.

items.fix: Cook the barberries in butter for a full 2 to 3 minutes with the sugar. They should plump and soften slightly. The sugar is small in quantity but important
items.cause: No sugar added or not cooked long enough to plump

items.fix: Use very hot (not boiling) water and steep for at least 10 minutes. Grind the saffron strands first between your fingers to release more colour
items.cause: Saffron steeped in cold or lukewarm water

items.fix: The steam from the water and the fat from the butter create the crust. Increase to 50 g butter and 3 tablespoons water for a more reliable result
items.cause: Not enough butter and water in the base
items.symptom: Rice is dry and separate but not crispy

items.fix: The chicken needs a gentle covered braise. Keep the lid on for the full cooking time and check the liquid level, add water if the pan looks dry
items.cause: Braised without enough liquid or lid not tight

content.text: Zereshk polo ba morgh is one of the great party dishes of Iranian home cooking. Cooks make it for guests, for Nowruz (Persian New Year), and for any day worth marking. The saffron gold, the ruby barberries, and the snowy rice make it as pretty as it is good. Barberries grow wild in Iran and Central Asia. They turn up in both savoury and sweet Persian dishes for their bright, sharp tang. The dish shows the Persian way with rice at its best: slow, careful, and built on the contrast between the soft steamed rice on top and the golden crust below.
